draft.introduction: Diogenes Laertius’s Lives of the Eminent Philosophers (3rd century CE) preserves the most complete ancient biography of Pythagoras. This passage describes his doctrines on the soul, transmigration, and the mathematical ordering of the cosmos.

translation.text: Pythagoras held that the soul is immortal and that it transmigrates into other kinds of living things. He was the first to declare that the soul, bound to the wheel of necessity, is born into different creatures at different times. He said that all living things should be regarded as akin.
translation.original_text: τὴν ψυχὴν ἔλεγεν ἀθάνατον εἶναι, εἶτα μεταβάλλειν εἰς ἄλλα γένη ζῴων

translator_notes.note: Diogenes Laertius is a late and often unreliable source, but his account synthesizes earlier traditions from Aristoxenus, Dicaearchus, and Timaeus. The doctrine of metempsychosis attributed here to Pythagoras became central to Platonic and Neoplatonic psychology.
